stories.title: Longpoint Partners Buys Miami-Dade Warehouses for $39M
stories.excerpt: Private equity firm Longpoint Partners has paid $38.8 million for a 121,579-square-foot industrial complex in Miami-Dade County. The property includes three warehouses, two totaling 94,500 square feet at 7200 Northwest 84th Avenue and the third spanning 27,079 square feet at 8251 Northwest 70th Street. All three are in Medley about half a mile west of the Palmetto Expressway. The sale is equal to about $319 a square foot. The small-bay warehouses are fully leased to about a dozen tenants, according to the Business Journals, which first reported the sale. The sellers, Martha and Ruben Artiles, have owned the 5.2-acre properties since the 1980s and 1990s. They could not be reached for comment. Longpoint Partners has been an active player in the South Florida industrial market. In 2023, the Boston-based firm paid $260 million for a 25-building industrial portfolio, totaling nearly 1.4 million square feet. The following year, it bought a 26-building last-mile industrial portfolio from Blackstone for $331 million. A representative for Longpoint did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Julia Echikson can be reached at jechikson@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Longpoint Partners buys 121,579 SF Miami-Dade industrial complex for $38.8M ($319/SF).
Three fully leased warehouses in Medley; firm owns $591M South Florida industrial portfolio.
Reflects continued PE demand for stabilized, leased last-mile and small-bay assets in metro.
stories.summary: Longpoint Partners acquired a 5.2-acre, fully leased three-building industrial complex in Medley, Miami-Dade for $38.8 million ($319/SF). The deal reinforces the firm's aggressive South Florida industrial strategy; it has deployed $591 million in the region since 2023. The purchase signals sustained investor appetite for stabilized, income-producing industrial real estate in supply-constrained submarkets.
stories.impact: Limited direct bearing on multifamily development. Industrial capital concentration in South Florida does not displace multifamily financing or development; if anything, it validates the metro's fundamentals and attracts more diverse investor capital to the market overall.

stories.title: Related Group’s Hollywood Live Local project draws suit from residents, nonprofit
stories.excerpt: Hollywood residents are jumping into the brawl over Florida’s developer-friendly Live Local Act with a lawsuit to block a planned beachfront tower.  Kathleen DiBona and the newly formed nonprofit Keep Public Lands Public is suing the City of Hollywood and an affiliate of Related Group over the proposed project at 1301 South Ocean Boulevard, claiming it violates a deed restriction on the site and should go before a public referendum, the South Florida Business Journal reported.  Related Group has a 99-year lease on the 4-acre site fronting a public beach, with plans to build a luxury condo and community center. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Related Group's Hollywood Live Local project (84 workforce units, 117 market-rate) faces lawsuit from residents.
Plaintiffs claim deed restriction and city charter bar 50+ year lease without voter referendum; case in Broward Circuit.
Live Local Act exemption from rezoning may not shield from ballot/deed restrictions in Florida.
stories.summary: Related Group's 201-unit mixed-use project at 1301 South Ocean Boulevard in Hollywood, approved under Florida's developer-friendly Live Local Act in May, is now under legal challenge by residents and a newly formed nonprofit. The lawsuit alleges violation of a deed restriction on the public beachfront site and claims city charter requires a voter referendum for leases of park/recreational land exceeding 50 years. Related obtained Live Local Act exemption from rezoning in exchange for 84 workforce units at 120% AMI.
stories.impact: Live Local Act approvals in South Florida may face extended litigation delays and unexpected legal costs, even post-rezoning. Developers betting on Live Local streamlining should model contingency for 12–24 month legal challenges and cost escalation. Hollywood market timing risk is now material for Related and competitors.

stories.title: Nahla’s $1B Raleigh Miami Beach expansion proposal secures blessing from board despite height concerns
stories.excerpt: Nahla Capital moved one step closer to securing approval for tweaks that will allow the developer to increase the size of the proposed Raleigh Miami Beach, a Rosewood-branded luxury condo and hotel project that’s been in the works for years.  Nahla acquired the 3-acre oceanfront assemblage at 1775, 1757 and 1751 Collins Avenue in October for $270 million, taking over from developer Michael Shvo and his partners.  At a Miami Beach Planning Board meeting on Tuesday, the board voted 5 to 1 to recommend approval of comprehensive plan and code amendments that will allow the developer to increase the floor […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Nahla Capital secures board approval to boost Raleigh Miami Beach FAR from 2.0 to 2.5 (+66,000 SF).
Project now ~332,000 SF; 52 condos, <80 hotel rooms; $1B total investment, on 3-acre oceanfront site.
Redevelopment timeline extended; fifth ownership change in 15 years; final city approvals still pending.
stories.summary: Nahla Capital won Miami Beach Planning Board approval (5–1 vote) to amend the Raleigh Miami Beach comprehensive plan and zoning, raising the floor-area ratio from 2.0 to 2.5. The move adds two floors and 66,000 SF to the luxury Rosewood-branded condo-hotel on a 3-acre Collins Avenue oceanfront site acquired by Nahla in October 2022 for $270 million. The $1B project will deliver 52 residential units and fewer than 80 hotel rooms; full city sign-off remains outstanding.
stories.impact: Nahla's FAR relief signals Miami Beach's willingness to densify premium oceanfront product and supports condo-hotel hybrid models. The project's fifth ownership in 15 years underscores prior underwriting or entitlement risks; accelerated approvals by Nahla reduce carry cost risk for future oceanfront assemblies but set precedent for density negotiation.

stories.title: Meet the developers behind the Pfizer building conversion
stories.excerpt: New York City turned its eyes towards the former Pfizer building in Midtown East Tuesday morning, but not for the reason the developers would’ve hoped. Reports emerged before 8 a.m. of debris falling from the construction site at 235 East 42nd Street, arguably the country’s most notable office-to-residential conversion post-pandemic to unfold. As of midday, concerns about the safety of the building remain as columns within the building buckle, forcing evacuations and street closures in and around the property. It’s not the first notable incident at the project, which commanded real estate’s attention long before pedestrians were forced to steer […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Pfizer building office-to-residential conversion (235 East 42nd Street, Midtown East) halted due to construction safety.
Structural columns buckling; debris fallen; Metro Loft's Nathan Berman and co-developer David Werner overseeing project.
Would surpass 25 Water Street's 1,320 units as largest U.S. residential conversion if completed.
stories.summary: The Pfizer headquarters conversion at 235 East 42nd Street in Midtown East was forced to suspend work after structural columns buckled and debris fell, triggering street closures. The project, led by Metro Loft Management's Nathan Berman and David Werner, faced safety inspections; the scale (would exceed 1,320 units when complete) and historic structure amplify reputational and financial risk. Berman has faced prior recapitalization challenges at other conversion projects.
stories.impact: Conversion projects in Midtown East now face heightened scrutiny and likely extended permitting timelines; lenders may tighten diligence on historic/structural conversion deals. The Pfizer incident reinforces execution risk premium on large-scale office conversions in constrained downtown submarkets; developers with unproven conversion track records will face capital cost pressure.

stories.title: Chase Bank Branch Garners Manhattan’s Sixth-Highest Price Per Square Foot Since 2023
stories.excerpt: Net lease real estate advisory and investment platform SURMOUNT has completed a $13-million sale of a Chase Bank branch located at 1295 Madison Ave. in the Carnegie Hill neighborhood. The firm represented both buyer and seller. The 3,000-square-foot ground-floor bank branch traded at a 4.74% cap rate, or $4,300 per square foot. The sale price per square foot ranks as the sixth highest achieved for a retail sale in Manhattan since 2023, a list that includes tenants such as Prada and Van Cleef & Arpels, according to SURMOUNT. The sale marks the latest chapter in a continued relationship between SURMOUNT and Patriot Real Estate Holdings, for whom the firm served as advisor on both the original acquisition in 2024 and its subsequent disposition. “Closing the sixth highest retail price per square foot in Manhattan since 2023 on a sub-3,000-square-foot bank branch speaks to the trophy nature of this asset and the depth of our buyer network,” said Anthony Bird, VP, investments at SURMOUNT. The post Chase Bank Branch Garners Manhattan’s Sixth-Highest Price Per Square Foot Since 2023 appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Chase Bank branch at 1295 Madison Ave, Carnegie Hill sells for $13M ($4,300/SF) at 4.74% cap rate.
Sixth-highest retail price per SF in Manhattan since 2023; trophy ground-floor retail asset in prime neighborhood.
Market signals continued trophy-asset price resilience despite broader retail headwinds.
stories.summary: SURMOUNT closed the sale of a 3,000-SF Chase Bank branch at 1295 Madison Avenue in Carnegie Hill for $13 million, or $4,300/SF, at a 4.74% cap rate. The transaction ranks sixth-highest for retail per-square-foot pricing in Manhattan since 2023, on par with luxury flagship tenants like Prada and Van Cleef & Arpels.
stories.impact: Minimal multifamily impact. Trophy retail pricing in Manhattan's best neighborhoods underscores flight-to-quality and institutional land scarcity; it does not signal ground-floor retail fundamentals for mixed-use or new multifamily product. Confirms that brand-name ground-floor anchors command premium valuations independent of broader retail disruption.

stories.title: Marcus & Millichap Brokers Self-Storage Property Deal in Chicago
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap completed the sale of a brand-new, 916-unit Public Storage property in Chicago, Illinois.“Above-average development complexity has constrained new self-storage supply, contributing to Chicago’s status as one of the industry’s strongest self-storage rental rate markets,” said Jeffrey Herrmann, senior director of investments. “Those market fundamentals continue to attract investors seeking high-quality assets in supply-constrained locations.”Herrmann and Sean Delaney, investment specialists in Marcus & Millichap’s Chicago Oak Brook office, marketed the property on behalf of the seller, an Illinois-based LLC. Delaney procured the buyer, a Texas-based LLC. Public Storage, located at 5251 N. Kedzie Ave., was completed in 2024 and consists of 916 climate-controlled self-storage units totaling 72,899 rentable square feet. The institutional-quality facility is situated in a densely populated area with a predominantly renter-occupied housing base and benefits from strong visibility along North Kedzie Avenue. Hear from the West Coast’s Leading Industrial Real Estate Experts | August 20Gain firsthand insight from the executives, investors, and developers navigating today’s industrial market. From shifting capital flows and debt trends to evolving occupier demand and development strategies, take part in the conversations shaping the future of industrial real estate. Register now: www.connectindustrialwest2026.com The post Marcus & Millichap Brokers Self-Storage Property Deal in Chicago appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Marcus & Millichap brokered sale of 916-unit brand-new Public Storage facility in Chicago.
Self-storage supply constraints cited; Chicago listed among strongest self-storage rental-rate markets.
Reflects investor appetite for supply-constrained, high-yield non-residential asset classes in Midwest.
stories.summary: Marcus & Millichap completed the sale of a newly constructed 916-unit Public Storage facility in Chicago. Broker commentary highlighted above-average development complexity constraining new self-storage supply and positioning Chicago as one of the industry's strongest rental-rate markets, attracting capital to supply-constrained assets.
stories.impact: Limited direct multifamily relevance. Self-storage capital flows do not compete with multifamily debt or equity. Chicago's self-storage supply constraints may indirectly signal strong land absorption and limited infill opportunities, potentially raising land costs for multifamily near transit or urban cores.

stories.title: Milhaus Receives Approval to Develop Kansas City Affordable Apartments
stories.excerpt: Milhaus has received approval from the Missouri Housing Development Commission for Low-Income Housing Tax Credits to advance Linwood & Troost Apartments in Kansas City. The project will convert two vacant lots at 3200 Troost Ave. into a 100% affordable residential development featuring two four-story buildings with 194 units. The project marks Milhaus’s first Low-Income Housing Tax Credit development in Kansas City. Funding for the project includes 4% federal tax credits, a tax abatement from Kansas City’s Planned Industrial Expansion Authority, and $1.5 million awarded from the Kansas City Housing Trust Fund. Milhaus will develop both four-story buildings concurrently. Construction is expected to begin by the end of 2026, with completion anticipated in approximately 2028. Located on the Troost MAX transit corridor, the development includes transit-supportive infrastructure such as wide sidewalks and designated loading areas. Milhaus is serving as both developer and general contractor on the project. The post Milhaus Receives Approval to Develop Kansas City Affordable Apartments appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Milhaus wins MHDC 4% LIHTC allocation for 194-unit affordable project (Linwood & Troost, Kansas City).
100% affordable development; tax abatement + $1.5M Housing Trust Fund grant; on transit corridor.
Milhaus' first LIHTC deal in KC; construction starts late 2026, completion 2028.
stories.summary: Milhaus received Missouri Housing Development Commission approval for 4% federal tax credits to finance Linwood & Troost Apartments, a 194-unit, 100% affordable conversion of two vacant lots in Kansas City. The project combines LIHTC, a municipal tax abatement, and $1.5 million from Kansas City's Housing Trust Fund. The transit-oriented development (Troost MAX corridor) will feature two four-story buildings; construction begins late 2026.
stories.impact: LIHTC availability and Kansas City tax incentive structures support entry of established developers (Milhaus) into the affordable market. Success here signals viable development-finance stacking in secondary Midwest markets; may attract more class-A developers to build affordable supply near transit, reshaping competitive dynamics and potentially moderating market-rate rent growth in Kansas City.

stories.title: Targus Relocates Headquarters to BGO-Owned Business Park
stories.excerpt: CBRE facilitated an 84,041-square-foot industrial lease for Targus at Anaheim Concourse, located at 1206 N. Miller St. in Anaheim. Targus will relocate its corporate headquarters to Anaheim, upgrading to a newly constructed Class A facility, owned by BGO. Sean Ward, Brian DeRevere and Ryan Peterson. of CBRE represented both the tenant and the landlord. The space is part of the Anaheim Concourse business park. It features a modern industrial design with 32-foot clear heights, an ESFR sprinkler system, a secured truck court and direct access to the region’s major freeway network. “This lease checked all the boxes, providing a modern Class A building with the functionality, location and image needed to support the company’s next chapter,” said Ward. “We’re continuing to see occupiers prioritize quality facilities in established infill markets, and this transaction is another example of companies making strategic real estate decisions that position them for long-term growth while staying connected to their employees, customers and the communities they serve.” Connect Industrial West | August 20 | Irvine, CAOn August 20, Connect Industrial West will bring together the owners, investors, developers, brokers, lenders, and occupiers driving industrial real estate across the Western U.S. Join top decision-makers for an afternoon of market insights, dealmaking opportunities, and networking with the industry’s most influential players. Register now: www.connectindustrialwest2026.com The post Targus Relocates Headquarters to BGO-Owned Business Park appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Targus relocates headquarters to Anaheim Concourse: 84,041 SF Class A industrial, 32-foot clear heights.
New facility replaces existing HQ; BGO owner; CBRE arranged; signals occupier flight to modern infill facilities.
Trend: corporates prioritizing quality, location, freeway access, and employee/customer connectivity.
stories.summary: Targus signed an 84,041-SF industrial lease at Anaheim Concourse (1206 N. Miller Street) for its corporate headquarters relocation. The newly constructed Class A facility features 32-foot clear heights, ESFR sprinklers, secured truck court, and direct freeway access. CBRE represented both tenant and landlord.
stories.impact: Corporates relocating to Class A industrial parks near freeways signal preference for modern, flexible space over legacy HQs; this may depress older industrial conversion/repositioning opportunities in secondary Orange County submarkets but validates newer infill product rents and lease terms in Anaheim area.

stories.title: Joseph Kavanagh Co. Completes Sale of HQ, Manufacturing Facility
stories.excerpt: Joseph Kavanagh Co. has completed the sale of its corporate headquarters and manufacturing facility at 8100 Lynhurst Road in Dundalk, Maryland, to Titan Steel Corporation for $2.3 million. MacKenzie Commercial Real Estate Services Senior Vice President and Principal Andrew Meeder, SIOR, and Matthew Curran, together with Real Estate Advisor Alton Curry, represented Joseph Kavanagh Co. in the sale of the nearly 19,000-square-foot building in Dundalk, containing a mix of office, industrial, and warehouse space. Established in 1866 by Joseph Kavanagh, the company began as a coppersmith shop and later transitioned into a metal-bending and rolling company. The 2.27-acre site, located at the intersection of Lynhurst Road and North Point Road, fronts Interstate 696 and features 14- to 16.5-foot ceilings, two drive-in loading docks, approximately 2,500 square feet of office space, and a fenced outdoor storage yard. 8100 Lynhurst Road is positioned approximately five miles from Interstate 95 and the Dundalk Marine Terminal, and is less than 20 miles from BWI Airport. The post Joseph Kavanagh Co. Completes Sale of HQ, Manufacturing Facility appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Joseph Kavanagh Co. sells HQ/manufacturing at 8100 Lynhurst Road, Dundalk, Maryland to Titan Steel for $2.3M.
19,000 SF office/industrial/warehouse building; 2.27 acres; near I-696, BWI, marine terminal.
Sale by 159-year family company reflects strategic asset redeployment, not distress.
stories.summary: Joseph Kavanagh Co., a 159-year-old coppersmith/metal-bending manufacturer, sold its Dundalk, Maryland headquarters and 19,000-SF manufacturing facility to Titan Steel Corporation for $2.3 million. The 2.27-acre site offers mixed office, industrial, and warehouse space with 14–16.5-foot ceilings, two drive-in docks, and proximity to I-95, I-696, BWI Airport, and the Dundalk Marine Terminal.
stories.impact: Minimal multifamily relevance. Reflects industrial real estate consolidation by manufacturing end-user (Titan Steel); does not signal capital flows, development risk, or supply dynamics affecting multifamily markets in Baltimore-Dundalk submarket.

stories.title: “You get what you pay for”: Fort Lauderdale will pursue $217M city hall project
stories.excerpt: After briefly considering buying and retrofitting an existing building into a city hall, Fort Lauderdale reverted to its original plan to develop from the ground up.  Commissioners voted 3-2 on Thursday in favor of having a developer build a $217.1 million city hall, which would cost Fort Lauderdale $15.8 million annually for 30 years, the South Florida Sun-Sentinel reported. FTL City Hall Partners LLC, an entity with a development team that includes Core Construction and the Stiles family’s eponymously named firm based in Fort Lauderdale, will be the developer.  The vote came after commissioners delayed proceeding earlier this year due […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Fort Lauderdale Commission approves $217.1M new city hall; FTL City Hall Partners LLC (Core + Stiles family) to develop.
Annual cost $15.8M for 30 years ($474M total); represents $50M reduction from initial $268M design-build estimate.
Commission vote 3-2; approval is preliminary; city may still pursue existing-building purchase.
stories.summary: Fort Lauderdale's city commission voted 3-2 to proceed with a $217.1 million new city hall developed by FTL City Hall Partners LLC (Core Construction and Stiles Family Firm partnership). The project carries a 30-year debt service and operations cost of $15.8 million annually ($474M total), reflecting a $50 million reduction from the original $268 million design-build proposal. The vote was preliminary; the city retained option to pursue buying an existing building.
stories.impact: Limited direct multifamily bearing. Municipal capital deployment in Fort Lauderdale does not constrain private multifamily development financing or land supply; if anything, large civic projects signal downtown reinvestment interest and may support mixed-use or Class-A multifamily development near city center.

stories.title: Affinius Capital Provides $188M Refi for N.J. Mixed-Use Apartment Complex
stories.excerpt: A joint venture among PCCP, Claremont Development, and Stanbery Development Group has secured $188 million to refinance the District 15Fifteen, a 498-unit multifamily property in Parsippany, N.J., Commercial Observer can first report. Affinius Capital provided the debt, which will finance the lease-up and stabilization of the property, as it opened only last month. Cushman & Wakefield’s Chuck Kohaut arranged the transaction. David Greenburg, Affinius Capital’s co-head of debt originations, described District 15Fifteen in a statement as a “high-quality asset” and noted that the Parsippany submarket benefits from “compelling” fundamentals stemming from a persistent lack of supply across the multifamily and retail spaces. “District 15Fifteen combines thoughtful design and a robust amenity offering in a supply-constrained, transit-oriented New Jersey submarket, exactly the kind of mixed-use property we look to finance,” he said. Located at 1515 Route 10 East in Parsippany — an affluent New Jersey suburb about 30 miles west of New York City — District 15Fifteen sits on nearly 13 acres of land and features three different buildings. A four-story residential building holds 262 units, while a five-story building holds 236 units. A third building is devoted entirely to retail. The entire complex holds 58,800 square feet of retail space and includes an 853-space parking garage. Amenities include a fitness center, multiple courtyards, a pool, a roof deck, and a dog spa. Brian Pascus can be reached at bpascus@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Affinius Capital refinances District 15Fifteen (Parsippany, NJ) with $188M; 498 units, just opened.
PCCP/Claremont/Stanbery JV; financing backs lease-up and stabilization; supply-constrained NJ submarket cited.
Parsippany transit-oriented; multifamily/retail supply gaps supporting debt terms.
stories.summary: A joint venture among PCCP, Claremont Development, and Stanbery Development Group secured $188 million from Affinius Capital to refinance District 15Fifteen, a 498-unit mixed-use multifamily property in Parsippany, New Jersey that opened last month. The refinance backs lease-up and stabilization. Affinius cited compelling submarket fundamentals: persistent lack of multifamily and retail supply and strong transit orientation.
stories.impact: Parsippany's supply-constrained multifamily submarket supports strong lease-up velocity and attractive debt terms for new supply. Developers with identified Parsippany-area sites or off-market land should accelerate entitlements; new supply will command rents supporting double-digit returns and tight refinancing timelines, reducing carry risk.

stories.title: PwC’s Move to Century City Is Another Blow to Downtown L.A.
stories.excerpt: The Los Angeles office market has shown measurable signs of recovery in recent months, but Downtown L.A. just suffered another high-profile setback. In a further extension of the larger flight-to-quality trend, accounting giant PricewaterhouseCoopers (PwC) is set to relocate from Downtown L.A. to Century City when its current lease expires in 2028, according to the Los Angeles Times. The firm signed a 15-year lease reportedly valued at roughly $200 million for approximately 150,000 square feet at Irvine Company‘s 2121 Avenue of the Stars in Century City. PwC will vacate its 114,000 square feet at the 52-story tower at 601 South Figueroa Street downtown, where its logo has been for years. PwC said the move was centered on growth and accessibility rather than an indictment of Downtown L.A. PwC’s Andy Sofield told the Times the Century City location provides additional space while offering stronger access to transportation corridors and airports for its clients and employees. He also noted the move returns the firm to a neighborhood where it previously maintained offices more than two decades ago. The move comes after Greater L.A. recorded nearly 4 million square feet of office leasing during the second quarter, according to Savills, which is 15 percent more than the prior quarter and nearly 8 percent above the same period last year. Leasing volume also exceeded the market’s five-year quarterly average by 22 percent. But PwC’s move also adds to a list of marquee tenants that have either reduced their space or left downtown behind. For example, Deloitte, KPMG and Wedbush Securities have all moved away from the city’s financial district in recent years, while Bank of America recently signed the region’s largest office transaction of the first half at Century Park Plaza. Century City has continued separating itself from the rest of the markets in Southern California. Savills reported the neighborhood commanded the region’s highest average ask
bullets: PwC relocates HQ from Downtown LA to Century City (Irvine Company); 150,000 SF; 15-year lease ~$200M.
Vacates 114,000 SF at 601 South Figueroa (52-story); move effective 2028.
High-profile exodus from Downtown LA; continues 'flight-to-quality' trend away from urban core.
stories.summary: PricewaterhouseCoopers signed a 15-year lease at Irvine Company's 2121 Avenue of the Stars in Century City for approximately 150,000 SF, valued at roughly $200 million, and will vacate its 114,000-SF space at 601 South Figueroa in Downtown Los Angeles when the current lease expires in 2028. PwC cited growth and accessibility; the move exemplifies a broader 'flight-to-quality' trend.
stories.impact: PwC's exit signals ongoing Downtown LA office distress and reduced urban-core appeal for large employers. Loss of 114,000 SF of quality office space in Downtown may depress adaptive-reuse (office-to-residential) conversion economics in the 52-story tower; conversely, Century City's appeal to major tenants will support Class-A office rents and may constrain multifamily development opportunity in Westside infill markets due to land cost competition.

stories.title: Manufacturing, Big-Box Leasing Fuel U.S. Industrial Reset in 2026
stories.excerpt: The U.S. industrial market is on stronger footing after the first half of 2026, with stronger leasing activity, calmer development compared to the pandemic-era highs, and a new wave of manufacturing investment reshaping demand. National asking rents climbed 1.8 percent year-over-year to $9.74 per square foot per year on a triple-net basis, despite elevated vacancy and rising sublease availability, according to a report from Savills. After two years of sustaining record-high deliveries, the national vacancy rate finished the second quarter at 8.2 percent, unchanged from the first quarter, and just 10 basis points higher than a year ago. Industrial leasing hit 491 million square feet through the first half of the year, which is 27 percent more than the first six months of 2025. The numbers represent the third-strongest first-half leasing total on record behind the pandemic boom years of 2021 and 2022. Deals for larger spaces — particularly leases exceeding 750,000 square feet — are driving activity nationally as big distributors and logistics users regain confidence after months of tariff and supply chain uncertainty, per Savills. As such, quarterly net absorption more than doubled year-over-year to 53.3 million square feet, while first-half absorption was 28 percent higher than during the same period in 2025. Meanwhile, industrial construction has also continued to reset. The national pipeline ticked up modestly to 320 million square feet, less than half the 782 million-square-foot peak in late 2022. Deliveries dropped to 49.4 million square feet during the second quarter from more than 82 million square feet at this point last year. At the same time, industrial demand is diversifying. Logistics remains the sector engine, but manufacturing is growing significantly. Savills tracked nearly 66,000 manufacturing jobs announced over the trailing 12 months through May, accompanied by almost $50 billion in planned capital investment. And, instead of electric vehicle manufac
bullets: U.S. industrial leasing H1 2026: 491M SF (+27% YoY); vacancy 8.2%; asking rents +1.8% YoY to $9.74/SF NNN.
Large-format leases (750K+ SF) driving activity; manufacturing wave reshaping demand vs. pandemic highs.
Supply moderation post-2023; third-strongest H1 leasing on record behind 2021–2022 boom.
stories.summary: U.S. industrial leasing totaled 491 million SF in the first half of 2026, up 27% year-over-year and representing the third-strongest H1 leasing total on record. National asking rents climbed 1.8% YoY to $9.74/SF (NNN basis). Vacancy remained flat at 8.2% Q1 to Q2. Large-format deals (750K+ SF) and a new wave of manufacturing investment are driving demand as deliveries moderate from pandemic-era peaks.
stories.impact: Industrial supply normalization and manufacturing resurgence reduce logistics-real-estate competition for capital and land. However, continued wage inflation and freight-hub infrastructure investment (especially near ports and interstates) may raise construction costs and land values in key industrial nodes, indirectly raising multifamily development costs in adjacent infill markets (e.g., LA, NJ, Chicago logistics nodes).

stories.title: BH, Apollo Cos buy out Related Group’s stake in Aventura mixed-use project
stories.excerpt: BH Group and Apollo Companies bought out Related Group’s stake in an Aventura development site slated for a mixed-use project, The Real Deal has learned. The Aventura City Center project at 2999 Northeast 191st Street is approved for a residential tower, a hotel and 26,000 square feet of retail space. The development is situated south of the Aventura Mall and near the Brightline station. BH and Related bought the 4.5-acre site, home to the Aventura View office building, for $51 million in 2022 and launched sales for Viceroy Residences Aventura there in early 2025. Plans call for a 23-story multifamily […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: BH Group and Apollo Companies buy out Related Group's stake in Aventura City Center development.
Approved for 248-unit residential tower, 153-room hotel, 26,000 SF retail; near Brightline, Aventura Mall.
Viceroy Residences pre-sales launched early 2025; original land purchase $51M in 2022.
stories.summary: BH Group and Apollo Companies acquired Related Group's stake in Aventura City Center, a mixed-use development at 2999 Northeast 191st Street approved for a 23-story, 248-unit residential tower; a 13-story, 153-room Marriott-branded hotel; and 26,000 SF of retail. The 4.5-acre site was purchased by Related and BH for $51 million in 2022. Viceroy Residences pre-sales commenced in early 2025.
stories.impact: Related's exit from the Aventura project (retaining no equity) may signal capital redeployment away from hospitality-hybrid multifamily or margin compression on the asset class. BH's continued South Florida expansion signals strength in South Florida multifamily and mixed-use fundamentals; competition for transit-oriented, hotel-adjacent sites near Brightline stations will likely intensify, supporting land and entry valuations.

stories.title: Bravo Property Trust Lends $68M on New Jersey Waterfront Apartments
stories.excerpt: Skyrock Capital has landed $68 million of construction financing for the development of a luxury waterfront multifamily project in Bayonne, N.J., Commercial Observer has learned. Bravo Property Trust supplied the loan for Skyrock’s planned 180-unit Bayonne Luxury Waterwalk apartment development. Elan Goldberg, co-head of originations at Bravo, originated the debt. “Bayonne’s waterfront is attracting serious long-term investment, and we were proud to give this sponsor the certainty to build,” Aaron Krawitz, CEO of Bravo Property Trust, said in a statement. Skyrock, under the entity Bayonne Luxury Waterwalk, acquired the 2.3-acre waterfront site along Newark Bay at 219 West Fifth Street for $4.55 million in 2019, Jersey Digs previously reported. Two floors of the six-story building will be utilized for parking with the property featuring amenities that include a swimming pool and fitness center, according to Jersey Digs. Skyrock Capital did not immediately return a request for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Bravo Property Trust provides $68M construction loan to Skyrock Capital for Bayonne Luxury Waterwalk (180 units).
Waterfront development in Bayonne, NJ; 2.3-acre site at 219 West Fifth Street; six stories, parking on two floors.
Waterfront NJ market attracting long-term investment; favorable lender appetite for stabilized multifamily.
stories.summary: Bravo Property Trust provided $68 million in construction financing to Skyrock Capital for Bayonne Luxury Waterwalk, a 180-unit luxury multifamily project on a 2.3-acre Newark Bay waterfront site in Bayonne, New Jersey. The six-story building includes two floors of parking and amenities such as pool and fitness center. Skyrock acquired the site for $4.55 million in 2019.
stories.impact: Bayonne waterfront's emergence as multifamily investment target (strong construction lending appetite, long-term sponsor confidence) signals rising Jersey City submarket strength. Developers with Bayonne waterfront or adjacent Newark Bay assemblies should accelerate entitlements; waterfront premium and transit connectivity (PATH access) justify density and rent premiums supporting new-supply development.

stories.title: Namdar Group grabs additional financing in Jersey City
stories.excerpt: The Namdar Group will not be deterred from building in Jersey City, or scoring the requisite financing to do so. Joel Namdar’s company secured $180 million in debt from Affinius Capital for its project at 35 Cottage Street in the Journal Square neighborhood, the Commercial Observer reported. The project was previously the recipient of a $152 million loan. The refinancing will support the 27-story, 564-unit luxury multifamily property going up a short walk from the local PATH station. Units at the building will range from studios to three-bedroom layouts, while commercial space is being earmarked for a Chabad synagogue, a […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Namdar Group secures $180M refinance from Affinius Capital for 35 Cottage Street (Journal Square, Jersey City).
27-story, 564-unit luxury tower; previously received $152M loan; completion targeted early 2027.
Namdar controls 1,126 additional units in concurrent Journal Square projects (29 Van Reipen, 612 Pavonia).
stories.summary: Namdar Group obtained $180 million in floating-rate, interest-only bridge financing from Affinius Capital (via Walker & Dunlop) for 35 Cottage Street, a 27-story, 564-unit luxury multifamily project in Jersey City's Journal Square neighborhood. The refinance builds on a prior $152 million loan. The project, one block from PATH station, is targeted for completion in early 2027 and will include studios to three-bedroom units plus commercial space (synagogue, preschool, party hall).
stories.impact: Namdar's control of ~1,800 units across three concurrent Journal Square projects (including $358M refinance in August for 1,126 units) signals sustained developer confidence in PATH-adjacent Jersey City submarkets and strong lender appetite for transit-oriented multifamily. Competitive supply density in Journal Square may compress rents in 2027–2028; developers entering Jersey City should prioritize differentiation (amenities, unit mix) and accelerate pre-leasing to capture early occupancy windows.
